articles.title: From 40°C heat to storm alerts: IMD confirms 'no heatwave' in April but rain to hit north India - Moneycontrol.com
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Ladakh
articles.full_text:
March 30, 2026 / 13:26 IST
Central and North India will witness rainfall in April instead of heatwave. (Image: X/ @jnmet) - IMD: No major April 2026 heatwaves in north, central India.
- Intermittent rain likely in Delhi-NCR, UP, Punjab, Haryana and Rajasthan.
- Western disturbances bring cooler temperatures and thunderstorms.
Did our AI summary help?
In a surprising turn after an early burst of summer heat, India may not experience major heatwaves in April 2026 across north and central regions. According to the latest weather updates many states expected to see below-normal temperatures and periodic rainfall. As multiple western disturbances continue to influence the region.
What did IMD confirm?
Story continues below Advertisement
The IMD has indicated that multiple active western disturbances are moving across northwestern India, preventing the quick build-up of heatwave conditions usually seen at this time of year.
This means that many Indian regions may continue to experience below-normal or near-normal daytime temperatures for the next few days.
Which Indian states are expected periodic rainfall?
According to the latest weather outlook, periodic rainfall is likely over in states like Delhi-NCR, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan and Himachal Pradesh.
Northern state region such as Uttarakhand and Jammu & Kashmir will also experience periodic rainfall in April. Some central and northeastern regions may also receive scattered showers and thunderstorms.
If no heatwave, what will happen instead?
Instead of dry, scorching winds, the weather pattern is shifting towards intermittent rain spells, thunderstorms and lightning. Scientists examined that gusty surface winds and possible hail would affect in some isolated areas. This will interrupt daytime heating and may even cause a 3–5°C dip in maximum temperatures across several northern states.
What is the science behind this weather shift?
The science of this shift is connected with the penetration of western disturbances. These events are the weather patterns, which start centering on the Mediterranean and move eastwards to the Indian subcontinent.
These systems bring moisture and cloud cover and decrease direct heating of the atmosphere by the sun and enhance atmospheric instability. This will trigger rainfall, thunderstorms and cool the land surface. They act as a natural atmospheric brake on heatwave formation.
A sharp contrast to early March heat
This forecast comes as a sharp contrast to the intense early-March heat spell, when several regions in north and west India saw temperatures pushing close to or above 40°C.
At that time, weather experts had warned of an early summer onset and possible April heatwaves. The present rain-bearing systems have temporarily reversed that trend, delaying the expected summer surge.
